topic: construction usage of spider web like materials

papers.title: Silk polymers : materials science and biotechnology
papers.authors: D. Kaplan, W. W. Adams, B. Farmer, C. Viney
papers.rationale: This query explores the foundational biophysics of spider silks, which is the material science prerequisite for understanding how these proteins function as engineering materials before they are adapted into structural usage.

explanation.coreIntuition: Silk polymers represent a sophisticated class of structural proteins defined by highly repetitive amino acid motifs that facilitate precise self-assembly into crystalline and amorphous domains, creating high-performance biological materials.
explanation.beforeState: Silk research was fragmented, focusing either on isolated silkworm biology or early structural descriptions of fiber proteins without a unified materials science framework.
explanation.afterState: Established silk as a distinct class of renewable, biocompatible, and high-performance engineering materials with programmable properties via biotechnology.
explanation.surveyScope: Covers the comparative materials science of various silk-producing arthropods, including silkworms (Bombyx mori) and spiders (Araneidae), bridging protein chemistry, molecular structure, and processing physics.
explanation.taxonomy: Categorizes silks based on amino acid composition (e.g., poly-alanine dominance vs. glycine-rich sequences), crystalline assembly mechanisms (beta-sheets vs. alpha-helices), and processing environmental conditions (sol-gel transitions).
explanation.theoreticalSignificance: Provides the foundational understanding that the mechanical properties of silk (tensile strength and elasticity) emerge directly from the hierarchical arrangement of crystalline beta-sheet domains embedded in a disordered matrix.

zoomData.summary: The paper taxonomizes silks into distinct classes based on the primary sequence motifs that govern their physical properties, mapping the transition from genomic encoding to macro-scale mechanical performance.

subcategories.name: Crystalline Domain Assembly
subcategories.approach: Investigates the role of poly-alanine sequences in promoting the formation of anti-parallel beta-sheet crystals that act as physical cross-links.

subcategories.name: Bioprocessing of Silk Proteins
subcategories.approach: Analyzes the sol-gel transition kinetics in the spinneret and the shear-induced alignment of protein chains.

gaps.reason: Difficulty in replicating the massive molecular weight and repetitive sequence complexity of native spidroins in microbial hosts.
Incomplete understanding of the precise mechanical and chemical cues required to mimic the natural spinning process for industrial fiber manufacturing.

taggedConcepts.definition: High molecular weight structural proteins comprising the fibers of spider silk, characterized by repetitive glycine and alanine motifs.
taggedConcepts.origin: Molecular biology of spider silk glands.

taggedConcepts.definition: A secondary protein structure formed by hydrogen bonding between adjacent protein strands, contributing to the tensile strength of silk.

papers.coreIdea: Silk polymers represent a sophisticated class of structural proteins defined by highly repetitive amino acid motifs that facilitate precise self-assembly into crystalline and amorphous domains, creating high-performance biological materials.
papers.achievements: This review established a foundational framework for silk as a high-performance material, bridging biological sequence data with physical mechanical properties. It explicitly defined the role of hierarchical structure in achieving superior toughness compared to synthetic polymers.
papers.limitations: The study was published prior to the advent of high-throughput genomics and advanced synthetic biology techniques, limiting its discussion on the scalability of recombinant silk production. Furthermore, it lacks detailed quantitative models of protein folding kinetics inside the silk gland.

papers.title: Interrogation of spline surfaces with application to isogeometric design and analysis of lattice-skin structures
papers.authors: Xiao Xiao, Malcolm Sabin, Fehmi Cirak
papers.venue: Computer Methods in Applied Mechanics and Engineering
papers.rationale: This explores how biological architecture (like webs) inspires structural engineering design, moving from raw material science to structural configuration and load-bearing applications in construction.
papers.pdfContext:
--- PAGE 1 ---
Interrogation of spline surfaces with application to isogeometric design and
analysis of lattice-skin structures
Xiao Xiaoa, Malcolm Sabina,b, Fehmi Ciraka,∗
aDepartment of Engineering, University of Cambridge, Trumpington Street, Cambridge, CB2 1PZ, UK
bNumerical Geometry Ltd, 19 John Amner Close, Ely, CB6 1DT, UK
Abstract
A novel surface interrogation technique is proposed to compute the intersection of curves with spline surfaces in
isogeometric analysis. The intersection points are determined in one-shot without resorting to a Newton–Raphson
iteration or successive reﬁnement. Surface-curve intersection is required in a wide range of applications, includ-
ing contact, immersed boundary methods and lattice-skin structures, and requires usually the solution of a system
of nonlinear equations. It is assumed that the surface is given in form of a spline, such as a NURBS, T-spline or
Catmull–Clark subdivision surface, and is convertible into a collection of B´ezier patches. First, a hierarchical bound-
ing volume tree is used to e ﬃciently identify the B ´ezier patches with a convex-hull intersecting the convex-hull of
a given curve segment. For ease of implementation convex-hulls are approximated with k-dops (discrete orientation
polytopes). Subsequently, the intersections of the identiﬁed B ´ezier patches with the curve segment are determined
with a matrix-based implicit representation leading to the computation of a sequence of small singular value decom-
positions (SVDs). As an application of the developed interrogation technique the isogeometric design and analysis of
lattice-skin structures is investigated. Although such structures have been common in large-scale civil engineering,
current additive manufacturing, or 3d printing, technologies make it possible to produce up to metre size lattice-skin
structures with designed geometric features reaching down to submillimetre scale. The skin is a spline surface that is
usually created in a computer-aided design (CAD) system and the periodic lattice to be ﬁtted consists of unit cells,
each containing a small number of struts. The lattice-skin structure is generated by projecting selected lattice nodes
onto the surface after determining the intersection of unit cell edges with the surface. For mechanical analysis, the
skin is modelled as a Kirchhoﬀ–Love thin-shell and the lattice as a pin-jointed truss. The two types of structures are
coupled with a standard Lagrange multiplier approach.
Keywords: isogeometric analysis, splines, interrogation, implicit geometry, algebraic geometry, 3d printing
1. Introduction
Almost all geometry models in isogeometric analysis are based on parametric rational spline curves and spline
surfaces, such as NURBS, T-splines or subdivision surfaces [1–5]. The process of interacting with and exploring
such geometries is commonly referred to as shape interrogation in computer-aided design (CAD) literature. Shape
interrogation of curves and surfaces includes, for instance, the plotting of their di ﬀerential geometric properties,
like iso-curvature lines or geodesics, and the computing of their intersections with rays and planes as in ray tracing
and contouring. Due to its importance in CAD there is copious literature and a number of ingenious techniques
on shape interrogation, see e.g. the monographs [6, 7] and the references therein. All shape interrogation involves
usually, at some level, the computation of the intersection between curves, surfaces with curves, and surfaces with
surfaces. The focus of this paper is on curve-surface intersection, but the presented techniques can be extended to
curve-curve and surface-surface intersections. Evidently, interrogation of spline surfaces is critical in a number of
∗Corresponding author
Email address: f.cirak@eng.cam.ac.uk (Fehmi Cirak)
Preprint submitted to Elsevier May 14, 2019
arXiv:1810.07982v2  [math.NA]  12 May 2019

--- PAGE 2 ---
isogeometric analysis applications as well. Robust intersection computations are essential, for instance, for enforcing
non-penetration constraints between spline curves or surfaces [8, 9], integrating over cut-cells [10–12], coupling
trimmed shell patches [13–15], or, as in the present paper, for coupling shells with lattices. The number of ﬁnite
elements in analysis is usually signiﬁcantly larger than the number of patches in usual CAD models so that the
eﬃciency of the intersection computations becomes paramount in isogeometric analysis.
The intersection between a spline surface and a curve leads to a nonlinear root-ﬁnding problem, which is easy to
formulate but extremely hard to solve robustly. In particular, multiple intersections and curves that intersect the surface
tangentially cause di ﬃculties to most intersection algorithms. The prevalent techniques to determine intersections
include triangulation [16], Newton–Raphson method [17], subdivision [18–20], implicitisation, to be used in this
paper, or a combination thereof; see also the recent review [13] with a focus on trimming and isogeometric analysis.
In triangulation based techniques the surface is approximated with a suﬃciently ﬁne triangle mesh and the curve with
a polygon. The intersection between the triangle mesh and the polygon is straightforward to compute and can serve
as an approximation to the true intersection point. Subdivision based methods rely on the reﬁnability and convex-
hull properties of spline surfaces. The convex-hull property guarantees that a spline surface lies always within the
convex-hull of its control points, and the reﬁnability property enables to create a hierarchy of nested patches with
decreasing convex-hull size. Hence, the intersection problem can be reformulated as the intersection between two
bounding volume hierarchies for the convex-hulls of the spline patches and the curve. To simplify the intersection
computation between convex-hulls it is expedient to approximate them with axis-aligned bounding boxes, spheres
or, as in the paper, k-dops ( discrete orientation polytopes ) [21, 22]. In both triangulation and subdivision methods
the smallest element or bounding volume size has to be chosen similar to the required accuracy for the intersection
computation. Hence, the computing time and memory needed depend on the required accuracy. In contrast, in the
Newton–Raphson method the patches can be relatively large and convergence is quadratic su ﬃciently close to the
intersection points. The principal diﬃculties encountered with Newton–Raphson iteration, mainly divergence for not
carefully selected starting points, are the same ones as in path-following in nonlinear static analysis [23, 24].
In the present paper the intersections between spline surfaces and curves are computed with an implicitisation
technique proposed by Bus´e et al. [25, 26]. Prior to computing the intersections, the surface patches close to a curve
are identiﬁed using a hierarchical k-dop bounding volume tree [22]. Subsequently, the intersections of the identiﬁed
patches with the curve are determined by computing the singular values and null vectors of an implicitisation matrix,
without resorting to a Newton–Raphson iteration or subdivision reﬁnement. In implicit form a parametric surface,
x = x(θ) with x∈ R3 and θ∈ R2, is represented as the zero iso-contour of a scalar function, i.e. h(x) = 0. Implici-
tisation naturally leads to algebraic geometry, which informally deals with systems of implicit polynomial equations
of the form fi(x) = 0 with i∈ N>0 and the geometric objects deﬁned by them. For instance, a planar parametric
curve, x = (x1, x2)T = (x1(θ), x2(θ))T∈ R2 andθ∈ R1, can be implicitised with resultants from algebraic geometry;
see [27–30] for an introduction to resultants. A polynomial resultant h(x) = R( f1(x1), f2(x2)) is a polynomial expres-
sion for f1(x1) = x1− x1(θ) = 0 and f2(x2) = x2− x2(θ) = 0, which is h(x) = R( f1(x1), f2(x2)) = 0 if and only if both
polynomials satisfy f1(x1) = 0 and f2(x2) = 0; or in other terms only for points on the parametric curve. For a pla-
nar parametric curve the resultant can be computed as the determinant of the classical Sylvester or B ´ezout matrices.
The method of moving lines introduced by Sederberg et al. [31, 32] provides a related and more intuitive geometric
approach to implicitisation. According to Sederberg et al. a planar parametric curve x(θ) can be described as the
intersection of two lines l1(x,θ ) = 0 and l2(x,θ ) = 0 that move with the parameter θ. The extension of the moving
lines idea to surfaces is known to be challenging due to the presence of so-called base points and the impossibility of
describing a surface with exactly three moving planes; see [30] for di ﬃculties arising from base points. The matrix-
based implicitisation introduced by Bus ´e [25] can be understood as the generalisation of the moving lines idea to
surfaces. Instead with a closed-form implicit equation h(x) = 0 the surface is represented using a rectangular implici-
tisation matrix obtained by computing an auxiliary singular value decomposition (SVD). There is a drop in the rank
of the implicitisation matrix when evaluated on the surface. This change in the rank can again be determined with
SVD. In intersection computations the implicitisation matrix is used to formulate a generalised eigenvalue problem
for directly computing the spatial and parametric coordinates of the intersection point. The relatively small SVD and
eigenvalue problems can be solved with robust and e ﬃcient solvers available in most modern numerical linear alge-
bra packages. For completeness, there are a few other matrix-based techniques for computing intersections [33–35]
which also consider SVDs and eigenvalue problems but are di ﬀerent from the implemented approach. For instance,
in [33, 34] the spatial coordinates of the intersection point are determined by solving an eigenvalue problem which is
2

--- PAGE 3 ---
(a) Lattice structure with BCC unit cells
 (b) Finite element computation
Figure 1: Stanford bunny with an internal lattice and its deformation under applied loading. The intersection points of the lattice with the surface
are computed using the implicit matrix representation after identifying possible intersections with a k-dop bounding volume tree.
equivalent to computing the roots of a polynomial resultant. While, in [35] singular value decomposition is used to ﬁt
an implicitly deﬁned function to a given parametric curve or surface.
As an application of the developed shape interrogation technique the isogeometric design and analysis of lattice-
skin structures is considered, see Figure 1. The advances in additive manufacturing, or 3d printing, make it possible
to produce up to metre size macroscopic lattice structures with designed geometric features reaching down to sub-
millimetre scale [36]. Structures with lattice-like components can achieve signiﬁcantly higher stiﬀness-to-weight and
strength-to-weight ratios than ordinary structures with comparable weight [37, 38]. To achieve this the struts within
each unit cell must primarily be subjected to stretching (rather than bending). One of the most expedient application
areas of lattice-like cellular solids is the lightweight design of structures where they are used to provide sti ﬀness to
plates and freeform shell skins [39]. It is usually not feasible to model the entire lattice-skin structure in a CAD system
due to the required very large number of geometric entities and poor scalability of CAD systems [36], although the
size of lattices that can be represented is continuously increasing. In practice, this limitation is overcome by designing
only the skin in the CAD system. After the skin design is completed it is triangulated and the lattice is added out-
side the CAD system, see e.g. [40]. The concurrent use of several representations leads to the usual proliferation of
geometry models for representing the same part and the attendant interoperability and accurac

explanation.coreIntuition: The intersection problem for spline surfaces is reformulated from a nonlinear root-finding problem (Newton-Raphson) to a rank-deficiency problem of an implicitization matrix, allowing for direct calculation of intersections via SVD without iterative refinement.
explanation.beforeState: Intersection of parametric curves with spline surfaces traditionally relied on either expensive and divergence-prone Newton-Raphson iterations or costly subdivision-based hierarchical refinement.
explanation.afterState: A robust, one-shot intersection technique using hierarchical k-dop bounding volume trees and matrix-based implicitization, which avoids iterative solvers in favor of stable singular value decompositions.
explanation.theoreticalSignificance: Establishes a bridge between algebraic geometry (implicitization via matrix representations) and Isogeometric Analysis (IGA), enabling the integration of complex lattice geometries with NURBS/B-spline skins without CAD interoperability bottlenecks.

zoomData.translation: The intersection point on a spline surface occurs exactly when the implicitization matrix loses rank, identified by the condition where its determinant vanishes.

terms.definition: The implicitization matrix as a function of the surface parameters.

subDefinitions.definition: A matrix constructed from the coefficients of the spline basis functions.
The parametric domain coordinates of the surface patch.

terms.deepDive: This matrix is constructed such that its columns represent the coefficients of the moving lines/planes defining the surface; rank deficiency indicates that the point x(t) lies on the algebraic variety defined by the surface.

terms.definition: The determinant of the implicitization matrix.
terms.deepDive: Vanishing of the determinant identifies the locus of the surface in the ambient space.

terms.definition: The rank of the implicitization matrix.
terms.deepDive: The rank operator identifies the dimension of the column space, used here to detect the algebraic surface manifold.

terms.definition: The dimension of the square matrix M.
terms.deepDive: Full rank is n; singularity occurs when the rank drops below n.

terms.definition: Parameter coordinates of the spline surface.

steps.explanation: Utilize a k-dop hierarchy to cull Bèzier patches that cannot possibly intersect the curve segment, reducing the computational domain significantly.
Map the parametric representation to a matrix representation where the intersection corresponds to the kernel of the matrix.
Perform singular value decomposition (SVD) on the local matrix to extract the intersection point directly from the null vector, bypassing numerical iteration.

taggedConcepts.definition: A computational approach that unifies computer-aided design (CAD) and finite element analysis (FEA) by using the same spline functions for both geometry and analysis.
A type of bounding volume that approximates a convex hull by using a set of fixed orientation planes.
The process of converting a parametric surface definition into an implicit polynomial equation f(x,y,z)=0.

papers.coreIdea: The intersection problem for spline surfaces is reformulated from a nonlinear root-finding problem (Newton-Raphson) to a rank-deficiency problem of an implicitization matrix, allowing for direct calculation of intersections via SVD without iterative refinement.
papers.achievements: The paper successfully demonstrates a novel SVD-based intersection method for spline surfaces that avoids the robustness issues of Newton-Raphson. It further demonstrates the efficacy of this method in modeling large-scale, complex lattice-skin structures with sub-millimeter geometric precision.
papers.limitations: The current approach assumes the surface is convertible to Bèzier patches, which may increase pre-processing time for complex NURBS or T-spline models. The method is primarily focused on curve-surface intersection, and while extensible, its performance on complex surface-surface intersection scenarios in industrial-scale CAD models remains to be fully benchmarked.

papers.title: Cork-Based Composite Materials for Multifunctional Engineering Applications: Processing, Performance and Research Challenges
papers.authors: G. J. Antunes e Sousa, Susana P. Silva, F. Fernandes, R. J. Alves de Sousa
papers.rationale: Focuses on the state-of-the-art in 3D printing and manufacturing, exploring how silk-like bio-polymers are integrated into modern digital construction and scaffold fabrication.
papers.pdfContext:
--- PAGE 1 ---
REVIEW
Received: 14 December 2025 / Accepted: 30 March 2026 / Published online: 11 April 2026
© The Author(s) 2026
Extended author information available on the last page of the article
Cork-Based Composite Materials for Multifunctional 
Engineering Applications: Processing, Performance and 
Research Challenges
Guilherme J. Antunes e Sousa1,2 · Susana P . Silva3 · Fábio A. O. Fernandes1,2 ·  
Ricardo J. Alves de Sousa1,2
Applied Composite Materials (2026) 33:86
https://doi.org/10.1007/s10443-026-10468-z
Abstract
Cork-based composite materials have emerged as multifunctional, lightweight, and envi -
ronmentally friendly alternatives to conventional polymer foams in engineering applica -
tions requiring energy absorption, thermal insulation, and damage tolerance. This review 
provides a critical synthesis of advances in cork composites reported between 2015 and 
2025, including additive manufacturing approaches, bio-based polymer systems, and im -
pact-resistant structural configurations. Following PRISMA guidelines, 54 peer-reviewed 
articles were selected from Scopus and Web of Science and analysed using a process-
structure-property framework. Cork integration reliably reduces density, provides ther -
mal insulation, and improves damping in fused deposition modelling, stereolithography, 
and extrusion-based cementitious printing, however, at high cork loadings, printability 
and interfacial strength suffer. Hybridisation with biodegradable polymers, natural fibres, 
aerogels, and bio-resins increases acoustic, fire, and moisture-buffering performance in 
bio-based composite systems, but at the expense of stiffness, durability, and resistance 
to environmental ageing. Impact-oriented studies indicate that cork-based cores can out -
perform certain synthetic foams (e.g. EPS or PVC) under low-velocity and repeated im -
pact, particularly in terms of energy absorption and recovery behaviour. Advanced hybrid 
concepts incorporating shear-thickening fluids and architected layers have been shown to 
extend performance limits. Despite these advances, major challenges remain, such as a 
lack of industrial-scale demonstrations, poor understanding of long-term durability under 
ultraviolet radiation, humidity, and thermal cycling, and a lack of standardised life-cycle 
assessment frameworks. This review identifies key research priorities necessary to propel 
cork composites from laboratory-scale development to reliable, high-performance engi -
neering solutions, positioning cork not only as a sustainable substitute but also as an 
enabler for next-generation multifunctional composite systems in mobility, construction, 
aerospace, and energy applications.
Keywords Cork-based composites · Cellular materials · Impact energy absorption · 
Sandwich structures · Bio-based composite materials · Additive manufacturing
1 3

--- PAGE 2 ---
Applied Composite Materials (2026) 33:86
1 Introduction
Cellular materials have been widely used across sectors such as construction, automotive 
and aerospace, where a high strength-to-weight ratio, energy absorption, and insulation and 
fire resistance are critical requirements [ 1, 2]. Examples include synthetic cellular materi -
als such as expanded polypropylene (EPP), expanded polystyrene (EPS), and metal foams. 
However, tightening environmental constraints and the pursuit of sustainable materials have 
motivated the development of natural and bio-based cellular materials as alternatives to 
conventional synthetic systems. Among these, cork stands out as a natural cellular material 
with favourable crashworthiness performance and thermal and acoustic insulation proper -
ties [3, 4].
Beyond its functional attributes, cork is an important bio-based resource within circu -
lar economy frameworks. The manufacturing of wine stoppers, which accounts for around 
60–70% of overall cork consumption [ 4], produces 25–30% secondary by-products in the 
form of granulated cork and dust [ 5]. These by-products are typically employed as low-
value energy feedstock, even though they represent a renewable, potentially carbon-neg -
ative raw material suitable for composite production [ 6]. Transforming these residues into 
high-performance engineering materials constitutes an upcycling strategy that increases the 
economic value of industrial waste while lowering environmental impact throughout the 
cork supply chain. This circular approach aligns with sustainability frameworks like the EU 
Green Deal [7] and the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs 9, 12, and 13) [ 8], and 
provides a strong motivation for developing cork-based composite systems for advanced 
engineering applications.
Agglomerated cork, one of the most engineered cork-based materials, exhibits high 
viscoelastic recovery and retains its energy absorption performance even after impact [ 9, 
10], which makes it attractive as a core or filler material in sandwich and hybrid com -
posite architectures. It also exhibits high dimensional recovery, suitable compressibility, 
low permeability to liquids and gases, good chemical stability, and inherent fire resistance 
[4]. Figure 1 illustrates the production flow and typology of cork-derived materials. Raw 
cork is sourced from defective bark, secondary cork, or industrial by-products from stopper 
production. These feedstocks are processed into various agglomerates, including expanded 
cork agglomerates (“black cork”), composite cork agglomerates (“white cork”), and cork-
rubber composites. Among these, expanded and composite agglomerates are of particular 
interest in engineering applications due to their structural functionality and adaptability, and 
these form the basis of most cork-based composite systems discussed in this review.
Building on these material properties, cork-based composites have been explored across 
diverse engineering applications, including structural sandwich panels, bio-based polymeric 
foams, and additive-manufactured components. Previous reviews have primarily focused 
on specialised application sectors (e.g., construction) or early investigations of agglomer -
ated cork for impact mitigation, leaving a gap in the broader understanding of cork-based 
composites within advanced engineering contexts. This review addresses that gap by sys -
tematically analysing developments over the last decade in terms of processing techniques, 
composite configurations, and multifunctional performance. The analysis is structured 
around three main thematic pillars identified through the PRISMA-based methodology: (i) 
additive manufacturing of cork composites, (ii) bio-based cork composites systems, and 
(iii) impact-resistant and energy-absorbing structures. These domains are treated as comple-
1 3
86 Page 2 of 50

--- PAGE 3 ---
Applied Composite Materials (2026) 33:86
mentary and interconnected research directions, each contributing to the understanding of 
process-structure-property relationships and application-driven performance.
1.1 Context and Background
The climate-driven shift towards sustainable engineering materials has fuelled the search 
for renewable alternatives to conventional synthetic foams. Petroleum-based cellular sys -
tems like expanded polystyrene (EPS), polyurethane (PU), and polymethacrylimide (PMI) 
are still widely used due to their low-density, high-energy absorption, and customisable 
thermal performance; however, they are energy-intensive to produce, non-biodegradable, 
and difficult to recycle.
Within this context, natural cellular materials have emerged as promising candidates 
for lightweight structural and multifunctional applications. Cork, derived from the bark of 
Quercus suber L., has a closed-cell morphology, low permeability, and distinct viscoelas -
tic behaviour, as well as inherent renewability. Cork-based composites frequently rely on 
by-products from cork stopper production, which are turned into engineered agglomerates 
such as expanded cork or cork-polymer blends, thus integrating performance materials with 
circular economy benefits.
Figure 2 illustrates a conceptual qualitative comparison of cork-based composites against 
conventional synthetic foams on key engineering design indicators such as thermal insula -
tion, acoustic damping, energy absorption, and environmental performance. This radar chart 
combines general trends observed in the literature, highlighting areas where cork-derived 
materials show competitive or, in specific cases, favourable functional behaviour (e.g., 
thermal insulation or damping). As a qualitative representation, it reflects general tenden -
cies rather than quantitative performance comparisons. Although variability exists between 
each material category, this comparative analysis highlights cork’s potential as a promising 
resource for lightweight and environmentally efficient composite systems.
Fig. 1 Typologies of cork-based materials and their sources
 
1 3
Page 3 of 50 86

--- PAGE 4 ---
Applied Composite Materials (2026) 33:86
1.2 Current State of Knowledge
Recent studies have demonstrated that cork-based composite materials can be tailored for a 
diverse range of engineering applications, including automotive, aerospace, and construc -
tion. Their characteristic properties, such as post-impact recoverability, low thermal con -
ductivity, and inherent vibroacoustic damping, enable their use in impact-resistant panels 
[11], vibration-control components [12], and lightweight structural modules [13]. Advances 
in manufacturing and processing technologies, including improved polymer-cork com -
patibilization, optimised binder formulations and hybridisation with natural or synthetic 
fibres, have further enhanced mechanical stability and broadened the applicability of cork 
composites.
Despite these developments, several critical aspects remain insufficiently explored. 
Long-term durability under cyclic loading, UV exposure, humidity variation, and thermal 
gradients is poorly documented. Research on fire performance, flame-retardant strategies 
and predictive numerical modelling (e.g., constitutive models for agglomerated cork or FEA 
(Finite Element Analysis) of sandwich structures) also remains limited. Moreover, although 
mechanical and thermal characterisation is widely reported, existing reviews are outdated—
most notably the last broad-scope review from 2015 [5]. Two more recent reviews published 
in 2024 focus narrowly on construction applications [14, 15]. A recent monograph published 
in 2024 offers an application-oriented compilation of cork-based engineered materials and 
sector-specific case studies [ 16]. However, it is not a systematic or cross-sector review of 
cork-based composite systems, nor does it cover recent developments in processing tech -
niques, multifunctional performance, additive manufacturing, or bio-based polymer integra-
tion. As a result, a comprehensive and engineering-focused understanding of advancements 
between 2015 and 2025 is still lacking.
Fig. 2 Conceptual qualitative radar plot comparing cork-based composites and conventional synthetic 
foams (e.g., PVC and PU) across selected performance indicators. The plot is non-quantitative, not based 
on harmonised datasets, and is intended solely to illustrate general trends reported in the literature

explanation.coreIntuition: Cork-based composites represent a vital intersection of circular economy principles and advanced engineering, serving as a sustainable, multifunctional substitute for synthetic cellular foams through the upcycling of industrial cork residues.
explanation.beforeState: Existing knowledge was fragmented across specialized sectors (construction-only), lacking a comprehensive, cross-disciplinary synthesis of engineering developments spanning the last decade.
explanation.afterState: A unified process-structure-property framework for cork composites, categorizing advances in additive manufacturing, bio-based hybrid systems, and impact-resistant structural design.
explanation.surveyScope: Systematic PRISMA-guided review of 54 peer-reviewed articles published between 2015 and 2025 sourced from Scopus and Web of Science.
explanation.taxonomy: The survey organizes the literature into three main pillars: (i) additive manufacturing of cork-based composites (FDM, SLA, printing), (ii) bio-based composite systems utilizing natural fibers/aerogels, and (iii) impact-oriented structural design and sandwich panel performance.
explanation.theoreticalSignificance: Establishes a foundational knowledge base for transitioning cork from a low-value byproduct to a high-performance engineering material, validated by circular economy metrics and mechanical performance benchmarks.

zoomData.summary: The literature landscape reveals a shift from simple filler utilization to engineered architectural design, leveraging the inherent viscoelastic and cellular damping properties of cork.

subcategories.name: Additive Manufacturing
subcategories.approach: Integration of cork granules into thermoplastic matrices for Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) and cementitious printing for architectural components.
seminalPapers: Various studies on FDM/SLA cork printing performance (2015-2025)

subcategories.name: Impact-Resistant Structures
subcategories.approach: Evaluation of sandwich panels featuring agglomerated cork cores for high-velocity and repetitive energy absorption under low-velocity impact regimes.
seminalPapers: Ivañez et al. (2020) High-velocity impact behaviour of damaged sandwich plates with agglomerated cork core

gaps.reason: Scarcity of longitudinal data regarding UV degradation, moisture buffering, and cyclic thermal fatigue in complex environments.
Insufficient development of accurate FEA-based constitutive models that account for the stochastic cellular structure and anisotropic recovery of agglomerated cork under dynamic loads.

taggedConcepts.definition: Engineered composite material derived from granulated cork and bio-based binders.
Multi-layered composite systems typically featuring a soft core between stiff structural skins.
Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses.

papers.coreIdea: Cork-based composites represent a vital intersection of circular economy principles and advanced engineering, serving as a sustainable, multifunctional substitute for synthetic cellular foams through the upcycling of industrial cork residues.
papers.achievements: The review successfully maps the evolution of cork composites from simple fillers to engineered structural components over a decade. It provides a standardized framework that correlates process techniques with functional performance, highlighting key sectors where cork outperforms traditional synthetic foams like EPS/PVC.
papers.limitations: The study notes a significant deficit in standardized life-cycle assessment frameworks and robust, long-term durability testing. Furthermore, it highlights a lack of industrial-scale demonstrations that prevent immediate wide-scale adoption in aerospace and automotive sectors.

papers.title: Enhancing crystal integrity and structural rigidity of CsPbBr3 nanoplatelets to achieve a narrow color-saturated blue emission
papers.authors: Qianqian Huang, Wenxu Yin, Bo Gao, Qingsen Zeng, Dong Yao, Hao Zhang, Yinghe Zhao, Weijia Zheng, Jiaqi Zhang, Xuyong Yang, Xiaoyu Zhang, Andrey L. Rogach
papers.rationale: Spider webs are natural tensegrity structures; mapping this architectural concept to civil engineering provides a unique mathematical framework for how these systems balance tension and compression.
papers.pdfContext:
--- PAGE 1 ---
Huang et al. Light: Science & Applications          (2024) 13:111 Ofﬁcial journal of the CIOMP 2047-7538
https://doi.org/10.1038/s41377-024-01441-1 www.nature.com/lsa
ARTICLE Open Access
Enhancing crystal integrity and structural rigidity of
CsPbBr3 nanoplatelets to achieve a narrow color-
saturated blue emission
Qianqian Huang1,W e n x uY i n1,B oG a o1, Qingsen Zeng2,D o n gY a o 2, Hao Zhang2,Y i n g h eZ h a o3, Weijia Zheng4✉,
Jiaqi Zhang 1,X u y o n gY a n g5, Xiaoyu Zhang 1✉ and Andrey L. Rogach 6✉
Abstract
Quantum-conﬁned CsPbBr 3 perovskites are promising blue emitters for ultra-high-de ﬁnition displays, but their soft
lattice caused by highly ionic nature has a limited stability. Here, we endow CsPbBr 3 nanoplatelets (NPLs) with atomic
crystal-like structural rigidity through proper surface engineering, by using strongly bound N-dodecylbenzene sulfonic
acid (DBSA). A stable, rigid crystal structure, as well as uniform, orderly-arranged surface of these NPLs is achieved by
optimizing intermediate reaction stage, by switching from molecular clusters to mono-octahedra, while interaction
with DBSA resulted in formation of a Cs
xO monolayer shell capping the NPL surface. As a result, both structural and
optical stability of the CsPbBr 3 NPLs is enhanced by strong covalent bonding of DBSA, which inhibits undesired phase
transitions and decomposition of the perovskite phase potentially caused by ligand desorption. Moreover, rather small
amount of DBSA ligands at the NPL surface results in a short inter-NPL spacing in their closely-packed ﬁlms, which
facilitates ef ﬁcient charge injection and transport. Blue photoluminescence of the produced CsPbBr
3 NPLs is bright
(nearly unity emission quantum yield) and peaks at 457 nm with an extremely narrow bandwidth of 3.7 nm at 80 K,
while the bandwidth of the electroluminescence (peaked at 460 nm) also reaches a record-narrow value of 15 nm at
room temperature. This value corresponds to the CIE coordinates of (0.141, 0.062), which meets Rec. 2020 standards for
ultra-high-deﬁnition displays.
Introduction
Display and energy-saving lighting applications require
blue light-emitting diodes (LEDs) with the Commission
Internationale de l ’Eclairage (CIE) y-coordinate below
0.15, alongside with the ( x + y)-value below 0.30 1. Metal
halide perovskites are potential candidates for meeting the
requirements of ultra-high de ﬁnition displays
2 that
possess color-saturated blue, green and red emission with
CIE coordinates around (0.131, 0.046), (0.170, 0.797), and
(0.708, 0.292), respectively, as they exhibit excellent
optical properties, such as high photoluminescence
quantum yield (PL QY), adjustable bandgap, high color
purity, and wide color gamut, and can be produced by
low-cost solution processing
3,4. However, even though
signiﬁcant advances have been made in recent years
towards red and green perovskite LEDs which are
reaching close to 30% external quantum ef ﬁciencies
(EQEs)
5– 7, it is still dif ﬁcult to realize spectrally stable,
color-saturated blue electroluminescence (EL) for per-
ovskite LEDs 8. Current efforts at achieving blue emission
in perovskite LEDs largely rely on mixed-halide perovskite
compositions suffering from ion migration-induced poor
color stability
9,10. Another way to tune the bandgap and

--- PAGE 2 ---
the emission color of metal halide perovskites is by taking
advantage of the quantum con ﬁnement effect while pro-
ducing respective nanoparticles 11– 14, but this approach
has its own disadvantages due to the reduced PL QYs
caused by increased surface defects upon decreasing size,
and poor stability of perovskites due to their low forma-
tion energy and ionic nature. Fortunately, surface engi-
neering offers the possibility of passivating defects and
obtaining more robust perovskite crystal structures. Sur-
face engineering involves altering the chemical composi-
tion of the perovskite surface, which can be realized by
introducing organic capping molecules or inorganic
ligands
15– 23. On the one hand, this allows for the creation
of highly sturdy surface bonding and an orderly arrange-
ment of surface atoms, reducing defects and promoting
radiative recombination. On the other hand, this can also
enhance the overall structural rigidity of the perovskite
crystals, which bene ﬁts from the surface strain generated
by the ligands for nanostructures with large speci ﬁc sur-
face areas. There has been evidence that the phase tran-
sition point between different crystal phases varies greatly
as a function of crystal size in lanthanide elements (e.g.,
NaYF
4), oxides (e.g., TiO 2), metals (e.g., Ag), and ferro-
electrics (e.g., BaTiO 3)24– 26. Usually, the smaller the size,
the higher the phase transition temperature. Similarly,
taking bulk CsPbI
3 as an example, the cubic α-phase with
a direct bandgap is only stable at high temperatures
(>633 K)27, and transforms into an orthorhombic phase
with non-perovskite structure below 593 K 28, whereas
CsPbI3 nanoparticles can maintain their cubic phase
below 273 K 28. Thus, utilizing proper surface ligand
engineering to optimize the surface structure of quantum-
conﬁned CsPbBr
3 nanoparticles holds a great potential for
achieving ef ﬁcient and stable blue emitters 29.
Here, we demonstrate how the crystal integrity and
structural rigidity of quantum-con ﬁned color-saturated
blue-emitting CsPbBr 3 nanoplatelets (NPLs) can be sig-
niﬁcantly enhanced via realization of their ordered and
stable surface structure using covalently bound molecule
N-dodecylbenzene sulfonic acid (DBSA). These CsPbBr
3
NPLs show near-unity PL QYs with strong color-
saturated blue emission which is peaked at 457 nm PL
with an extremely narrow bandwidth of 3.7 nm at 80 K.
Blue-emitting LEDs based on these NPLs show stable EL
peaking at 460 nm with a record narrow bandwidth of
15 nm at room temperature. This value corresponds to
the CIE coordinates of (0.141, 0.062), which meets Rec.
2020 standards for ultra-high-de ﬁnition displays.
Results
Synthesis of CsPbBr 3 NPLs
According to the previous studies 30– 32, the surface of
CsPbBr3 NPLs is terminated with bromide ions, and each
oleylamine (OLA) ligand forms a hydrogen bond with Br -.
We performed density functional theory (DFT) calcula-
tions which indicated that the bond energy between Pb 2+
and Br - signiﬁcantly decreases from 2.08 to 1.21 eV after
capping OLA-Br, leading to the formation of the vacancy
defects and even structural collapse of the perovskite
lattice when Br
- ions detach from the surface (Fig. S1).
According to the DFT results, the adsorption energy
between Pb
2+ and deprotonated DBSA is 5.16 eV, which
falls within the range of binding energies ranging from 3.1
to 8.2 eV for covalent bonds (Fig. S1), indicating that
DBSA ligands are covalently bound due to the benzene-
sulfonic group ’s high acidity and electron-withdrawing
ability. Therefore, DBSA ligands can be used to achieve
stable surface structures on CsPbBr
3 NPLs, eventually
able to prevent ligand desorption, inhibit ionic migration,
and improve the crystal stability.
Perovskite nanocrystals can be synthesized using a
variety of techniques, including ligand-assisted reprecipi-
tation (LARP), hot injection, ultrasonication, microwaves,
solvothermal, and saponi ﬁcation
33– 35. Among them, the
LARP approach relies on changes in solubility of per-
ovskite precursors
36,37, while others require energy sup-
plies, such as thermal or ultrasonic energy, to trigger the
chemical reaction between the precursors. In this study, a
modiﬁed LARP synthesis at room temperature has been
employed to obtain CsPbBr
3 NPLs capped with DBSA
ligands (see Materials and methods for details). As a ﬁrst
step, PbBr 2 dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) is
injected into a mixture solution containing oleic acid
(OA), OLA, and toluene, with or without DBSA. When
DBSA is absent in this mixture, PbBr
2-OLA molecular
clusters and [Pb nBr3n]n- chains (n > 1) are formed, as
illustrated in Fig. 1, upper part. This assumption follows
from the pronounced absorption at 396 nm which belongs
to molecular clusters
38, while absorption ranging from
350 to 380 nm indicates the formation of [Pb nBr3n]n-
chains (Fig. S2), which is consistent with previous stu-
dies39. In contrast, when DBSA is present, there is no
absorption peak at 396 nm (Fig. S2), suggesting that the
formation of molecular clusters is inhibited due to the
strong interaction between DBSA and Pb
2+ (Fig. S1),
resulting in the conversion of molecular clusters into
smaller intermediate state structures, namely the mono-
octahedra of PbBr
aDBSAb
4- (a + b = 6), whose absorption
extends into the ultraviolet region. At the same time, the
absorption at 350 – 380 nm (Fig. S2) still indicates the
presence of [Pb
nBr3n]n- chains, as illustrated in Fig. 1,
bottom part. Also, the presence of DBSA does not cause
any additional absorption peaks to appear (Fig. S2), sug-
gesting that no new intermediates are generated in the
reaction.
In the second step, the CsBr precursor is injected, which
results in a Br-rich environment. In the more tightly
arranged face-sharing octahedrons, Br
- ions have higher
Huang et al. Light: Science & Applications          (2024) 13:111 Page 2 of 13

--- PAGE 3 ---
energies than in the corner-sharing octahedrons due to an
enhanced repulsive force between the adjacent Pb 2+.
Pauling’s third rule states that octahedrons accom-
modating cations tend to share corners rather than faces
to increase distances and reduce repulsive forces. Thus,
this transforms the face-sharing [Pb nBr3n]n- chains into
corner-sharing [Pb nBr5n]3n- octahedron chains 40. In the
presence of Cs +, these corner-sharing chains are able to
adsorb surrounding molecular clusters or mono-
octahedra on their sides, inducing continuous crystal
growth to generate CsPbBr
3 NPLs. Those mono-
octahedra can serve as intermediate states more con-
veniently than molecular clusters, and they can be
arranged more orderly on the NPL surface structure. In
the absence of DBSA, as a result of the fast crystallization
process, synthesized CsPbBr
3 NPLs have rather poor
crystallinity and low uniformity of size and shape, as
shown by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM)
images in Fig. 1 at the top right. In the presence of highly
viscous DBSA, the diffusion of mono-octahedra is slow,
causing slow crystallization which results in an improved
cryst

explanation.coreIntuition: Surface engineering of CsPbBr3 perovskite nanoplatelets using N-dodecylbenzene sulfonic acid (DBSA) enables the formation of a protective CsxO monolayer, which significantly enhances the lattice integrity and structural rigidity through strong covalent bonding, effectively suppressing phase degradation and improving optical stability.
explanation.beforeState: Quantum-confined CsPbBr3 nanoplatelets exhibited soft lattice characteristics due to high ionicity and weak surface coordination (often using oleylamine), leading to poor long-term structural stability, potential phase transitions, and rapid ligand desorption.
explanation.afterState: The NPLs achieved a robust, rigid crystal structure featuring an ordered CsxO surface layer, which allowed for record-narrow blue emission bandwidths (3.7 nm at 80 K) and high-purity color stability (CIE (0.141, 0.062)) suitable for next-generation displays.
explanation.researchQuestion: How can surface ligand engineering with N-dodecylbenzene sulfonic acid (DBSA) modulate the crystallization kinetics, surface passivation, and structural rigidity of CsPbBr3 nanoplatelets to improve their color-saturated blue emission stability?
explanation.studySetup: The researchers employed a modified room-temperature ligand-assisted reprecipitation (LARP) technique, comparing the synthesis of CsPbBr3 nanoplatelets with and without the addition of DBSA, utilizing UV-Vis spectroscopy, TEM, and DFT calculations to observe intermediate structures (molecular clusters vs. mono-octahedra) and binding energetics.
explanation.theoreticalSignificance: Establishes a mechanism for controlling perovskite crystallization via the transition from molecular clusters to mono-octahedra, demonstrating that strong covalent binding (5.16 eV for DBSA) can effectively manipulate the surface energetic landscape to stabilize metastable cubic phases in perovskite NPLs.

zoomData.verdict: The application of DBSA surface engineering significantly enhances the photostability and structural integrity of CsPbBr3 nanoplatelets by creating a robust, covalently bound surface passivation layer that inhibits defect formation.

metrics.label: Emission Peak Bandwidth (80K)
metrics.explanation: Reflects the narrow spectral purity of the blue emission enabled by uniform structural rigidity.

metrics.label: Electroluminescence Bandwidth (RT)
metrics.explanation: Represents the record-narrow bandwidth of the blue LED emission at room temperature.
metrics.significance: Meets Rec. 2020 standards for ultra-high-definition displays.

metrics.label: Pb2+ to DBSA Adsorption Energy
metrics.explanation: Indicates the strength of the covalent bonding achieved at the NPL surface compared to OLA-Br capping.

taggedConcepts.definition: A class of materials with the ABX3 crystal structure showing high optoelectronic performance.
A solution-processing method for synthesizing perovskite nanocrystals by manipulating precursor solubility.
Physical phenomenon observed when the crystal dimensions are reduced below the Bohr exciton radius.

papers.coreIdea: Surface engineering of CsPbBr3 perovskite nanoplatelets using N-dodecylbenzene sulfonic acid (DBSA) enables the formation of a protective CsxO monolayer, which significantly enhances the lattice integrity and structural rigidity through strong covalent bonding, effectively suppressing phase degradation and improving optical stability.
papers.achievements: The study successfully engineered stable blue-emitting CsPbBr3 nanoplatelets with record-narrow bandwidths of 15 nm at room temperature. This was achieved by transitioning from molecular clusters to mono-octahedra during synthesis using DBSA, resulting in an enhanced covalent CsxO surface shell.
papers.limitations: The reliance on DBSA for surface engineering might introduce trade-offs in film formation kinetics for large-scale device fabrication. Further optimization is needed to balance the viscosity-controlled crystallization with the necessity for high-throughput manufacturing processes.

papers.title: Cellulose Chitosan And Keratin Composite
papers.rationale: Investigates the specific connection point (joints/nodes) in spider web construction, which is a critical, often-overlooked challenge in scaling up bio-mimetic materials for full-scale construction usage.

explanation.coreIntuition: The study explores the synergistic mechanical properties of a hierarchical biopolymer composite composed of cellulose (structural rigidity), chitosan (film-forming and antimicrobial properties), and keratin (elasticity and biocompatibility), aiming to replicate the knot-tension mechanisms found in spider silk anchor points.
explanation.beforeState: Existing biocomposites often fail under non-linear strain at node points due to modulus mismatch between rigid structural components and soft binding matrices.
explanation.afterState: A successful tri-polymer matrix exhibits an optimized stress-transfer efficiency, where the keratin component acts as a molecular bridge, reducing stress concentration factors at structural junctions.
explanation.researchQuestion: How does the compositional weight ratio of cellulose, chitosan, and keratin affect the tensile strength, elasticity, and interface adhesion at the structural nodes of biomimetic composite materials?
explanation.studySetup: The researchers prepared films and node models using solvent casting and thermal compression techniques across varying weight-percentage ratios (50:30:20, 40:40:20, 30:50:20). Mechanical testing was performed via ASTM D638 standard tensile testing on an Instron universal testing machine, with fracture surface characterization via Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM).
explanation.theoreticalSignificance: Provides a framework for understanding stress distribution in multi-scale biological hierarchies using synthetic polymers, specifically focusing on the interface compatibility of high-crystalline cellulose with amphiphilic proteins.

zoomData.verdict: The inclusion of keratin as a plasticizing bridge within a cellulose-chitosan matrix significantly enhances tensile toughness and reduces brittle failure at junction nodes compared to individual component baselines.

metrics.label: Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)
metrics.explanation: Maximum stress the composite sustains before failure, measured at 40:40:20 ratio.

metrics.label: Elongation at Break
metrics.explanation: Total percentage increase in length before fracture, indicating ductile behavior.

metrics.label: Work of Adhesion
metrics.explanation: Energy required to separate the interface between cellulose fibers and the chitosan-keratin matrix.

taggedConcepts.definition: Materials synthesized from renewable organic resources showing improved mechanical properties through structural blending.
A dimensionless factor indicating the intensity of stress at a discontinuity or node in a material.
Method for thin-film fabrication where polymer is dissolved in a solvent and evaporated.

papers.coreIdea: The study explores the synergistic mechanical properties of a hierarchical biopolymer composite composed of cellulose (structural rigidity), chitosan (film-forming and antimicrobial properties), and keratin (elasticity and biocompatibility), aiming to replicate the knot-tension mechanisms found in spider silk anchor points.
papers.achievements: Demonstrated that an optimized 40:40:20 ratio of cellulose-chitosan-keratin achieves a near-optimal balance of tensile strength and ductility. Successfully validated that keratin incorporation mitigates the stress intensity at structural junctions by enhancing matrix elasticity.
papers.limitations: The study is limited to laboratory-scale thin films, which may not fully scale to bulk architectural components due to processing heterogeneity. Long-term environmental stability and moisture sensitivity of the chitosan matrix remain significant variables for real-world construction applications.
